content:
# Luke Chapter 18 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 110-111 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_018_page_001.jpg, LUK_018_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And he was telling them a parable, to the end that they ought always to pray and not to lose heart,

**2** saying, "There was a certain judge in a certain city who did not fear God and did not regard man.

**3** And there was a widow in that city, and she kept coming to him, saying, 'Avenge me of my adversary.'

**4** And for a time he was not willing; but after these things he said within himself, 'Even though I do not fear God nor regard man,

**5** yet because this widow causes me trouble, I will avenge her, lest by her continual coming she wear me out.'"

**6** And the Lord said, "Hear what the unrighteous judge says.

**7** And shall not God accomplish the avenging of his elect, who cry out to him day and night, and is he patient over them?

**8** I say to you that he will accomplish their avenging speedily. Nevertheless, when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith upon the earth?"

**9** And he spoke also this parable to certain ones who trusted in themselves that they were righteous and despised the rest:

**10** "Two men went up into the temple to pray, the one a Pharisee and the other a tax collector.

**11** The Pharisee, having stood, was praying these things to himself: 'God, I thank thee that I am not as the rest of men — extortioners, unrighteous, adulterers — or even as this tax collector.

**12** I fast twice in the week; I give tithes of all that I acquire.'

**13** But the tax collector, standing far off, would not even lift up his eyes to heaven, but was striking his breast, saying, 'God, be merciful to me, the sinner.'

**14** I say to you, this one went down to his house justified rather than that one. For everyone who exalts himself shall be humbled, and he who humbles himself shall be exalted."

**15** And they were bringing to him also the infants, that he might touch them; but the disciples, having seen it, rebuked them.

**16** But Jesus called them to himself, saying, "Permit the children to come to me, and do not forbid them, for of such is the kingdom of God.

**17** Truly I say to you, whoever does not receive the kingdom of God as a child shall by no means enter into it."

**18** And a certain ruler asked him, saying, "Good Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?"

**19** And Jesus said to him, "Why do you call me good? No one is good except one — God.

**20** You know the commandments: 'Do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not bear false witness, honor your father and mother.'"

**21** And he said, "All these things I have kept from my youth."

**22** And having heard this, Jesus said to him, "Yet one thing is lacking to you: sell all that you have and distribute to the poor, and you shall have treasure in heaven; and come, follow me."

**23** But having heard these things, he became exceedingly sorrowful, for he was very rich.

**24** And Jesus, seeing him become exceedingly sorrowful, said, "How difficultly shall those who have riches enter into the kingdom of God!

**25** For it is easier for a camel to enter through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter into the kingdom of God."

**26** And those who heard it said, "And who is able to be saved?"

**27** And he said, "The things impossible with men are possible with God."

**28** And Peter said, "Behold, we have left our own things and followed you."

**29** And he said to them, "Truly I say to you, there is no one who has left house or wife or brothers or parents or children for the sake of the kingdom of God,

**30** who shall not receive manifold more in this time, and in the age to come, eternal life."

**31** And having taken the twelve aside, he said to them, "Behold, we are going up to Jerusalem, and all the things that have been written through the prophets concerning the Son of Man shall be accomplished.

**32** For he shall be delivered over to the Gentiles, and shall be mocked, and shall be insulted, and shall be spat upon,

**33** and having scourged him, they shall kill him; and on the third day he shall rise."

**34** And they understood none of these things, and this saying was hidden from them, and they did not comprehend the things being said.

**35** And it came to pass, as he drew near to Jericho, a certain blind man was sitting beside the road, begging.

**36** And having heard a crowd passing through, he inquired what this might be.

**37** And they reported to him that Jesus the Nazarene is passing by.

**38** And he cried out, saying, "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!"

**39** And those who were going before rebuked him, that he should be silent; but he cried out much more, "Son of David, have mercy on me!"

**40** And Jesus, having stood still, commanded him to be brought to him. And when he had drawn near, he asked him,

**41** "What do you wish that I should do for you?" And he said, "Lord, that I may receive my sight."

**42** And Jesus said to him, "Receive your sight; your faith has saved you."

**43** And immediately he received his sight and was following him, glorifying God. And all the people, having seen it, gave praise to God.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **18:1 — "to the end that they ought always to pray" (pros to dein pantote proseuchesthai autous):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΡΟΣ ΤΟ ΔΕΙΝ ΠΑΝΤΟΤΕ ΠΡΟΣΕΥΧΕΣΘΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ ΚΑΙ ΜΗ ΕΝΚΑΚΕΙΝ. The construction ΠΡΟΣ ΤΟ + infinitive expresses purpose ("to the end that"). The verb ΔΕΙΝ (impersonal, "it is necessary") imposes moral obligation upon the act of prayer — this is not an invitation but a requirement. The adverb ΠΑΝΤΟΤΕ ("always, at all times") removes temporal limitation. The verb ΕΝΚΑΚΕΙΝ (variant spelling of ΕΓΚΑΚΕΙΝ, "to lose heart, to become weary") is compounded from ΕΝ + ΚΑΚΟΣ — literally "to be in a bad condition," hence to grow faint or despondent. The parable that follows is explicitly identified as didactic before it begins — Luke removes all ambiguity about its purpose. Prayer is framed not as a spiritual luxury but as the alternative to despair.

2. **18:2 — "a certain judge" (kritēs tis):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΡΙΤΗΣ ΤΙΣ ΗΝ ΕΝ ΤΙΝΙ ΠΟΛΕΙ ΤΟΝ ΘΕΟΝ ΜΗ ΦΟΒΟΥΜΕΝΟΣ ΚΑΙ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΝ ΜΗ ΕΝΤΡΕΠΟΜΕΝΟΣ. The judge is characterized by two negations: he does not ΦΟΒΕΙΣΘΑΙ ("fear") God and does not ΕΝΤΡΕΠΕΣΘΑΙ ("regard, have respect for") man. The verb ΕΝΤΡΕΠΩ in the middle voice means "to turn oneself toward" someone out of respect or shame — this judge turns toward no one. The double negation removes both vertical accountability (to God) and horizontal accountability (to society). He is a figure of absolute judicial autonomy — answerable to neither divine law nor public opinion. In the Jewish legal system, cases involving a single plaintiff (such as a widow) could be heard by a single judge, making this scenario historically plausible. The indefinite ΤΙΣ ... ΤΙΝΙ ("a certain ... a certain") universalizes the parable — this is not a specific city or judge but an archetype.

3. **18:3 — "Avenge me of my adversary" (ekdikēson me apo tou antidikou mou):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΚΔΙΚΗΣΟΝ ΜΕ ΑΠΟ ΤΟΥ ΑΝΤΙΔΙΚΟΥ ΜΟΥ. The verb ΕΚΔΙΚΕΩ means "to carry out justice, to avenge, to vindicate" — from ΕΚ + ΔΙΚΗ ("out of justice"). The widow does not ask for mercy but for justice; she has a legal claim and demands its execution. The noun ΑΝΤΙΔΙΚΟΣ ("adversary, opponent in a lawsuit") is a technical legal term — from ΑΝΤΙ + ΔΙΚΗ ("counter-justice"), one who brings or faces a suit. The imperfect ΗΡΧΕΤΟ ("she kept coming") indicates repeated, persistent visits — not a single request but a campaign of unrelenting petition. The widow's situation was precarious in the ancient world: without a male advocate, she was vulnerable to exploitation and dependent upon the integrity of judges whom Deuteronomy 10:17-18 charged to defend the fatherless and the widow.

4. **18:4-5 — "lest she wear me out" (hina mē hypopiazē me):** Sinaiticus reads ΙΝΑ ΜΗ ΥΠΟΠΙΑΖΗ ΜΕ. The verb ΥΠΟΠΙΑΖΩ is extraordinarily vivid — it derives from ΥΠΩΠΙΟΝ ("the area under the eye") and literally means "to strike under the eye, to give a black eye." Paul uses the same word in 1 Corinthians 9:27 for disciplining his body. Applied metaphorically here, it means "to wear out completely, to exhaust by persistent assault." The judge's capitulation is motivated not by justice but by self-interest — he acts to end the annoyance. The phrase ΕΙΣ ΤΕΛΟΣ ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΗ ("coming to the end" or "continually coming") in Sinaiticus reinforces the relentlessness of the widow's approach. The internal monologue (ΕΙΠΕΝ ΕΝ ΕΑΥΤΩ, "said within himself") reveals a man who acknowledges his own moral bankruptcy with perfect equanimity — he admits his godlessness as a simple fact, not a confession.

5. **18:6-8 — "the unrighteous judge" (ho kritēs tēs adikias):** Sinaiticus reads Ο ΚΡΙΤΗΣ ΤΗΣ ΑΔΙΚΙΑΣ — literally "the judge of unrighteousness," a Semitic genitive of quality equivalent to "the unrighteous judge." The application moves from lesser to greater (a fortiori): if an unjust judge yields to persistence, how much more will the righteous God avenge his chosen ones (ΤΩΝ ΕΚΛΕΚΤΩΝ ΑΥΤΟΥ, "his elect")? The phrase ΒΟΩΝΤΩΝ ΑΥΤΩ ΗΜΕΡΑΣ ΚΑΙ ΝΥΚΤΟΣ ("who cry out to him day and night") uses the genitive of time (ΗΜΕΡΑΣ ΚΑΙ ΝΥΚΤΟΣ) to denote unceasing petition. The verb ΜΑΚΡΟΘΥΜΕΙ ("is patient, is long-suffering") in Sinaiticus raises an interpretive question: does God delay his vengeance (testing patience) or is he patient on behalf of his elect (exercising patience toward their persecutors)? The formal translation renders "is he patient over them" to preserve the ambiguity. The final question — ΠΛΗΝ Ο ΥΙΟΣ ΤΟΥ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΥ ΕΛΘΩΝ ΑΡΑ ΕΥΡΗΣΕΙ ΤΗΝ ΠΙΣΤΙΝ ΕΠΙ ΤΗΣ ΓΗΣ ("when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith upon the earth?") — shifts abruptly from divine faithfulness to human faithfulness. The particle ΑΡΑ ("then, therefore") tinges the question with doubt. The article ΤΗΝ ΠΙΣΤΙΝ ("the faith") may refer specifically to the persistent, prayerful faith just described.

6. **18:9 — "who trusted in themselves that they were righteous" (tous pepoithotas eph' heautois hoti eisin dikaioi):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΟΥΣ ΠΕΠΟΙΘΟΤΑΣ ΕΦ ΕΑΥΤΟΙΣ ΟΤΙ ΕΙΣΙΝ ΔΙΚΑΙΟΙ ΚΑΙ ΕΞΟΥΘΕΝΟΥΝΤΑΣ ΤΟΥΣ ΛΟΙΠΟΥΣ. The perfect participle ΠΕΠΟΙΘΟΤΑΣ ("having trusted, being confident") denotes a settled state of self-assurance — these are people whose confidence in their own righteousness is established and ongoing. The verb ΕΞΟΥΘΕΝΕΩ ("to despise, to treat as nothing") — from ΕΞ + ΟΥΘΕΝ ("out of nothing") — means to reduce someone to nothing, to treat them as having zero value. The objects of this contempt are ΤΟΥΣ ΛΟΙΠΟΥΣ ("the rest, the remaining ones") — everyone else without exception. The two attitudes are presented as inseparable: self-righteousness necessarily produces contempt for others. Luke's editorial introduction identifies the target audience before the parable begins, an unusual narrative strategy that removes suspense but sharpens didactic focus.

7. **18:10 — "Two men went up into the temple" (dyo anthrōpoi anebēsan eis to hieron):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΥΟ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΙ ΑΝΕΒΗΣΑΝ ΕΙΣ ΤΟ ΙΕΡΟΝ ΠΡΟΣΕΥΞΑΣΘΑΙ. The verb ΑΝΕΒΗΣΑΝ ("went up") reflects the topography of Jerusalem — one always "goes up" to the temple mount. The ΙΕΡΟΝ denotes the temple complex generally, not the ΝΑΟΣ (the inner sanctuary). The infinitive of purpose ΠΡΟΣΕΥΞΑΣΘΑΙ ("to pray") establishes the common ground: both men enter for the same ostensible purpose. The Pharisee (ΦΑΡΙΣΑΙΟΣ) and the tax collector (ΤΕΛΩΝΗΣ) represent the two extremes of Jewish social standing — the scrupulously observant and the publicly sinful. The ΤΕΛΩΝΗΣ was not merely a tax collector but a toll-farmer who purchased the right to collect taxes for Rome, profiting from any excess extracted — a system that bred extortion and made the tax collector a byword for moral corruption and national betrayal.

8. **18:11 — "The Pharisee, having stood, was praying these things to himself" (ho Pharisaios statheis tauta pros heauton prosēucheto):** Sinaiticus reads Ο ΦΑΡΙΣΑΙΟΣ ΣΤΑΘΕΙΣ ΤΑΥΤΑ ΠΡΟΣ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΠΡΟΣΗΥΧΕΤΟ. The phrase ΠΡΟΣ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ("to himself") is grammatically ambiguous — it may modify ΣΤΑΘΕΙΣ ("having stood by himself," i.e., standing apart) or ΠΡΟΣΗΥΧΕΤΟ ("was praying to himself," i.e., his prayer was self-directed). The formal translation attaches it to the verb of praying, yielding the devastating implication that the Pharisee's prayer never reaches beyond himself — it is a monologue of self-congratulation addressed to God in name only. The imperfect ΠΡΟΣΗΥΧΕΤΟ ("was praying") suggests an extended, habitual prayer style. His thanksgiving (ΕΥΧΑΡΙΣΤΩ ΣΟΙ, "I thank thee") is formally correct — thanksgiving is proper in prayer — but its content is entirely comparative: he defines his righteousness not by what he is but by what he is not, and specifically by his superiority to others. The catalog of sins he disclaims (ΑΡΠΑΓΕΣ, "extortioners"; ΑΔΙΚΟΙ, "unrighteous"; ΜΟΙΧΟΙ, "adulterers") culminates in a specific comparison: Η ΚΑΙ ΩΣ ΟΥΤΟΣ Ο ΤΕΛΩΝΗΣ ("or even as this tax collector"). The demonstrative ΟΥΤΟΣ ("this one") is contemptuous — he points at a specific person present.

9. **18:12 — "I fast twice in the week; I give tithes of all that I acquire" (nēsteuō dis tou sabbatou, apodekatō panta hosa ktōmai):** Sinaiticus reads ΝΗΣΤΕΥΩ ΔΙΣ ΤΟΥ ΣΑΒΒΑΤΟΥ ΑΠΟΔΕΚΑΤΩ ΠΑΝΤΑ ΟΣΑ ΚΤΩΜΑΙ. The Mosaic law required fasting only on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:29); the Pharisee's twice-weekly fast (traditionally Monday and Thursday) represents voluntary supererogation — obedience beyond obligation. Similarly, the tithe law (Lev 27:30; Num 18:21-24) applied to agricultural produce; the Pharisee tithes ΠΑΝΤΑ ΟΣΑ ΚΤΩΜΑΙ ("all that I acquire") — extending the requirement to every form of income. Both claims are probably truthful; the Pharisee is not a hypocrite in the usual sense. His error is not that he lies about his piety but that he treats his supererogatory piety as grounds for divine approval and human contempt. The emphatic ΕΓΩ ("I") that pervades his prayer makes the self its own center of gravity.

10. **18:13 — "God, be merciful to me, the sinner" (ho Theos, hilasthēti moi tō hamartōlō):** Sinaiticus reads Ο ΘΕΟΣ ΙΛΑΣΘΗΤΙ ΜΟΙ ΤΩ ΑΜΑΡΤΩΛΩ. The verb ΙΛΑΣΚΟΜΑΙ (here aorist passive imperative ΙΛΑΣΘΗΤΙ) means "to be propitiated, to be merciful" — it belongs to the vocabulary of atonement and sacrifice, carrying the sense of divine wrath being turned aside. In the LXX, the related noun ΙΛΑΣΤΗΡΙΟΝ denotes the mercy-seat, the gold cover of the Ark of the Covenant where sacrificial blood was sprinkled on the Day of Atonement (Lev 16:2, 13-15). The tax collector's prayer thus implicitly invokes the entire sacrificial system: he asks not for lenient judgment but for propitiatory atonement. The article ΤΩ ΑΜΑΡΤΩΛΩ ("the sinner") is significant — not "a sinner" among many but "the sinner," as if he were the only one, or the chief one. His physical posture reinforces his words: ΜΑΚΡΟΘΕΝ ΕΣΤΩΣ ("standing far off") marks spatial distance from the holy place; ΟΥΚ ΗΘΕΛΕΝ ΟΥΔΕ ΤΟΥΣ ΟΦΘΑΛΜΟΥΣ ΕΠΑΡΑΙ ΕΙΣ ΤΟΝ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΝ ("he would not even lift up his eyes to heaven") marks the aversion of the guilty from the holy; ΕΤΥΠΤΕΝ ΤΟ ΣΤΗΘΟΣ ΑΥΤΟΥ ("he was striking his breast") is a gesture of grief and self-accusation. Every element of his prayer is the antithesis of the Pharisee's: where the Pharisee stood prominently and looked upward in confidence, the tax collector stands at a distance and looks downward in shame.

11. **18:14 — "this one went down justified rather than that one" (katebē houtos dedikaiōmenos eis ton oikon autou par' ekeinon):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΑΤΕΒΗ ΟΥΤΟΣ ΔΕΔΙΚΑΙΩΜΕΝΟΣ ΕΙΣ ΤΟΝ ΟΙΚΟΝ ΑΥΤΟΥ ΠΑΡ ΕΚΕΙΝΟΝ. The perfect passive participle ΔΕΔΙΚΑΙΩΜΕΝΟΣ ("having been justified") is the theological heart of the parable. ΔΙΚΑΙΟΩ means "to declare righteous, to acquit" — it is a forensic term from the law court, a verdict pronounced by the judge. The passive voice indicates divine action: it is God who justifies. The preposition ΠΑΡΑ with the accusative (ΠΑΡ ΕΚΕΙΝΟΝ) means "rather than, in comparison with" — the tax collector is justified rather than the Pharisee. The verb ΚΑΤΕΒΗ ("went down") corresponds to ΑΝΕΒΗΣΑΝ ("went up") in verse 10 — the physical descent from the temple mount mirrors the spiritual descent of the Pharisee and the spiritual ascent of the tax collector. The concluding maxim — ΟΤΙ ΠΑΣ Ο ΥΨΩΝ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΤΑΠΕΙΝΩΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ Ο ΔΕ ΤΑΠΕΙΝΩΝ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΥΨΩΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("for everyone who exalts himself shall be humbled, and he who humbles himself shall be exalted") — employs the divine passive: God is the unnamed agent who humbles and exalts.

12. **18:15 — "the infants" (ta brephē):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΑ ΒΡΕΦΗ. Luke uses ΒΡΕΦΟΣ ("infant, baby") rather than Mark's ΠΑΙΔΙΑ ("children") — ΒΡΕΦΟΣ denotes a newborn or very young child, still carried in arms. The word appears in Luke 1:41, 44 for the unborn John leaping in Elizabeth's womb, and in 2:12, 16 for the infant Jesus in the manger. The detail heightens the scene: these are not children old enough to walk to Jesus on their own but babies brought by their parents. The disciples' rebuke (ΕΠΕΤΙΜΩΝ ΑΥΤΟΙΣ) uses the imperfect, indicating ongoing, repeated attempts to turn the parents away.

13. **18:16-17 — "Permit the children to come to me" (aphete ta paidia erchesthai pros me):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΦΕΤΕ ΤΑ ΠΑΙΔΙΑ ΕΡΧΕΣΘΑΙ ΠΡΟΣ ΜΕ ΚΑΙ ΜΗ ΚΩΛΥΕΤΕ ΑΥΤΑ. Jesus shifts from ΒΡΕΦΗ to ΠΑΙΔΙΑ ("children") — a broader term encompassing young children generally. The double command is emphatic: ΑΦΕΤΕ ("permit," aorist imperative — a decisive allowance) and ΜΗ ΚΩΛΥΕΤΕ ("do not forbid," present imperative with negation — cease the ongoing hindrance). The reason clause ΤΩΝ ΓΑΡ ΤΟΙΟΥΤΩΝ ΕΣΤΙΝ Η ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ("for of such is the kingdom of God") does not mean children automatically possess the kingdom but that the kingdom belongs to those who share the characteristic disposition of children. Verse 17 specifies: ΟΣ ΑΝ ΜΗ ΔΕΞΗΤΑΙ ΤΗΝ ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΩΣ ΠΑΙΔΙΟΝ ("whoever does not receive the kingdom of God as a child"). The verb ΔΕΧΟΜΑΙ ("to receive, to welcome") casts the kingdom as something given, not achieved — and the child's posture is that of reception without merit, the same posture exemplified by the tax collector of the preceding parable.

14. **18:18-19 — "Good Teacher ... Why do you call me good?" (Didaskale agathe ... ti me legeis agathon?):** Sinaiticus reads ΔΙΔΑΣΚΑΛΕ ΑΓΑΘΕ ΤΙ ΠΟΙΗΣΑΣ ΖΩΗΝ ΑΙΩΝΙΟΝ ΚΛΗΡΟΝΟΜΗΣΩ. The address ΔΙΔΑΣΚΑΛΕ ΑΓΑΘΕ ("Good Teacher") was unusual in Jewish practice — rabbis were not typically addressed with the adjective ΑΓΑΘΟΣ, which was reserved for God. Jesus's counter-question ΤΙ ΜΕ ΛΕΓΕΙΣ ΑΓΑΘΟΝ ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ΑΓΑΘΟΣ ΕΙ ΜΗ ΕΙΣ Ο ΘΕΟΣ ("why do you call me good? No one is good except one — God") does not deny his own goodness but redirects the ruler's attention to the theological implications of his own address. If ΑΓΑΘΟΣ properly belongs to God alone, then calling Jesus ΑΓΑΘΟΣ either exceeds what the ruler intends or implies more than the ruler realizes. The ruler's question ΖΩΗΝ ΑΙΩΝΙΟΝ ΚΛΗΡΟΝΟΜΗΣΩ ("inherit eternal life") uses ΚΛΗΡΟΝΟΜΕΩ ("to inherit") — a word from the domain of inheritance law, treating eternal life as an estate to be received. The verb ΠΟΙΗΣΑΣ ("having done," aorist participle) reveals the ruler's assumption: eternal life is earned by doing something.

15. **18:20 — The commandments listed:** Sinaiticus reads ΜΗ ΜΟΙΧΕΥΣΗΣ ΜΗ ΦΟΝΕΥΣΗΣ ΜΗ ΚΛΕΨΗΣ ΜΗ ΨΕΥΔΟΜΑΡΤΥΡΗΣΗΣ ΤΙΜΑ ΤΟΝ ΠΑΤΕΡΑ ΣΟΥ ΚΑΙ ΤΗΝ ΜΗΤΕΡΑ. Jesus cites five commandments from the second table of the Decalogue — those governing human relationships. The order follows the LXX of Deuteronomy 5, where adultery precedes murder (differing from the Hebrew order in Exodus 20). Notably absent is the tenth commandment ("You shall not covet") — a commandment that, unlike the others, addresses internal desire rather than external action. Given that the ruler claims to have kept all the listed commandments (v. 21), the omission of the covetousness prohibition may be deliberately pointed: the one commandment he cannot claim to have kept is the one Jesus does not mention, yet it is precisely covetousness that his subsequent refusal will expose.

16. **18:22 — "Yet one thing is lacking to you" (eti hen soi leipei):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΤΙ ΕΝ ΣΟΙ ΛΕΙΠΕΙ. The adverb ΕΤΙ ("yet, still") and the numeral ΕΝ ("one") together create surgical precision — not many things, but one single deficiency. The verb ΛΕΙΠΩ ("to lack, to be wanting") in the present tense indicates an ongoing deficiency, not a recent development. The command unfolds in four stages: ΠΑΝΤΑ ΟΣΑ ΕΧΕΙΣ ΠΩΛΗΣΟΝ ("sell all that you have"), ΔΙΑΔΟΣ ΠΤΩΧΟΙΣ ("distribute to the poor"), ΚΑΙ ΕΞΕΙΣ ΘΗΣΑΥΡΟΝ ΕΝ ΟΥΡΑΝΟΙΣ ("and you shall have treasure in heaven"), ΚΑΙ ΔΕΥΡΟ ΑΚΟΛΟΥΘΕΙ ΜΟΙ ("and come, follow me"). The sequence moves from divestiture through charity to heavenly reward to personal discipleship. The final imperative ΑΚΟΛΟΥΘΕΙ (present imperative, "keep following") envisions not a single act but a continuous life of discipleship. The "one thing" that is lacking is not merely generosity but total reorientation — from self-possessed righteousness to dispossessed dependence.

17. **18:23 — "exceedingly sorrowful" (perilypos):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΕΡΙΛΥΠΟΣ ΕΓΕΝΗΘΗ. The adjective ΠΕΡΙΛΥΠΟΣ ("exceedingly sorrowful, deeply grieved") — from ΠΕΡΙ ("around, exceedingly") + ΛΥΠΗ ("grief") — denotes grief that surrounds and engulfs. The same word describes Jesus in Gethsemane (Mark 14:34). The explanatory clause ΗΝ ΓΑΡ ΠΛΟΥΣΙΟΣ ΣΦΟΔΡΑ ("for he was very rich") uses ΣΦΟΔΡΑ ("exceedingly, very much") to intensify ΠΛΟΥΣΙΟΣ ("rich"). Luke alone among the Synoptics adds ΣΦΟΔΡΑ, emphasizing the extremity of the ruler's wealth and thereby the extremity of the demand. The ruler does not argue, does not negotiate, does not ask for clarification — he simply grieves. His sorrow is the tribute that attachment pays to the truth it cannot obey.

18. **18:24-25 — "How difficultly ... a camel through the eye of a needle" (pōs dyskolōs ... kamēlon dia trymatos belonēs):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΩΣ ΔΥΣΚΟΛΩΣ ΟΙ ΤΑ ΧΡΗΜΑΤΑ ΕΧΟΝΤΕΣ ΕΙΣ ΤΗΝ ΒΑΣΙΛΕΙΑΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ ΕΙΣΠΟΡΕΥΟΝΤΑΙ. The adverb ΔΥΣΚΟΛΩΣ ("with difficulty, hardly") — from ΔΥΣ ("hard, ill") + ΚΟΛΟΝ ("food") — originally meant "hard to please with food," hence "difficult, reluctant." The phrase ΟΙ ΤΑ ΧΡΗΜΑΤΑ ΕΧΟΝΤΕΣ ("those having riches") uses ΧΡΗΜΑΤΑ ("possessions, money") — from ΧΡΑΟΜΑΙ ("to use"), denoting wealth as usable assets. The camel-and-needle image (v. 25) is deliberately grotesque: ΚΑΜΗΛΟΝ ("camel"), the largest animal in Palestine, versus ΤΡΗΜΑΤΟΣ ΒΕΛΟΝΗΣ ("eye of a needle"), the smallest domestic aperture. ΒΕΛΟΝΗ is a sewing needle (Luke's term; Matthew and Mark use ΡΑΦΙΣ, also a needle). Medieval attempts to interpret the "needle's eye" as a small gate in Jerusalem have no historical or linguistic basis — the image is pure hyperbole, asserting impossibility.

19. **18:26-27 — "The things impossible with men are possible with God" (ta adynata para anthrōpois dynata para tō Theō estin):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΑ ΑΔΥΝΑΤΑ ΠΑΡΑ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΙΣ ΔΥΝΑΤΑ ΠΑΡΑ ΤΩ ΘΕΩ ΕΣΤΙΝ. The audience's response ΚΑΙ ΤΙΣ ΔΥΝΑΤΑΙ ΣΩΘΗΝΑΙ ("and who is able to be saved?") reveals that they understood Jesus's words as universal, not merely applicable to the extremely wealthy — if the rich, who were conventionally regarded as blessed by God, cannot enter the kingdom, then salvation is impossible for anyone. Jesus's answer does not soften the impossibility but relocates it: ΑΔΥΝΑΤΑ ΠΑΡΑ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΙΣ ("impossible with men") affirms the human impossibility, while ΔΥΝΑΤΑ ΠΑΡΑ ΤΩ ΘΕΩ ("possible with God") asserts the divine possibility. Salvation is not difficult but impossible — apart from divine intervention. The preposition ΠΑΡΑ ("with, from the side of, in the estimation of") occurs twice with different datives, creating a symmetry that contrasts human incapacity with divine omnipotence.

20. **18:28 — "we have left our own things" (hēmeis aphentes ta idia):** Sinaiticus reads ΗΜΕΙΣ ΑΦΕΝΤΕΣ ΤΑ ΙΔΙΑ. Peter's statement is implicitly contrastive with the ruler's failure: where the ruler could not relinquish his wealth, the disciples have left ΤΑ ΙΔΙΑ ("their own things, their possessions"). The pronoun ΗΜΕΙΣ ("we") is emphatic — placed first for contrast. Luke's ΤΑ ΙΔΙΑ ("one's own things") is more restrained than Mark's enumeration of specific items left behind, but the claim is the same: the disciples have done what the ruler could not. Peter's statement is half question, half assertion — it leaves unstated but implied the question: "What then shall we receive?"

21. **18:29-30 — "manifold more in this time, and in the age to come, eternal life" (pollaplasiona en tō kairō toutō kai en tō aiōni tō erchomenō zōēn aiōnion):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΟΛΛΑΠΛΑΣΙΟΝΑ ΕΝ ΤΩ ΚΑΙΡΩ ΤΟΥΤΩ ΚΑΙ ΕΝ ΤΩ ΑΙΩΝΙ ΤΩ ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΩ ΖΩΗΝ ΑΙΩΝΙΟΝ. The reward is bifurcated across two ages: ΚΑΙΡΟΣ ΟΥΤΟΣ ("this time," the present age) and ΑΙΩΝ ΕΡΧΟΜΕΝΟΣ ("the age to come," the eschatological future). In the present, ΠΟΛΛΑΠΛΑΣΙΟΝΑ ("manifold more") — Luke uses this general term where Mark specifies "a hundredfold." The items left behind — ΟΙΚΙΑΝ ("house"), ΓΥΝΑΙΚΑ ("wife"), ΑΔΕΛΦΟΥΣ ("brothers"), ΓΟΝΕΙΣ ("parents"), ΤΕΚΝΑ ("children") — represent the full spectrum of human attachment. Luke alone includes ΓΥΝΑΙΚΑ ("wife") in this list, which has generated discussion: it does not endorse marital abandonment but reflects the potential cost of discipleship in a context of persecution, where allegiance to Christ could sever the marriage bond. In the age to come: ΖΩΗΝ ΑΙΩΝΙΟΝ ("eternal life") — the very thing the ruler asked about in verse 18 and could not obtain because he could not relinquish.

22. **18:31-33 — The third passion prediction:** Sinaiticus reads ΠΑΡΑΛΑΒΩΝ ΔΕ ΤΟΥΣ ΔΩΔΕΚΑ ΕΙΠΕΝ ΠΡΟΣ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ. This is the most detailed of Luke's three passion predictions (cf. 9:22; 9:44). Jesus takes the twelve aside (ΠΑΡΑΛΑΒΩΝ, "having taken aside") for private instruction. The phrase ΠΑΝΤΑ ΤΑ ΓΕΓΡΑΜΜΕΝΑ ΔΙΑ ΤΩΝ ΠΡΟΦΗΤΩΝ ("all the things written through the prophets") grounds the passion in scriptural necessity — the suffering is not arbitrary but prophetically determined. The catalog of sufferings is precise: ΠΑΡΑΔΟΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ΤΟΙΣ ΕΘΝΕΣΙΝ ("shall be delivered over to the Gentiles"), ΕΜΠΑΙΧΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("shall be mocked"), ΥΒΡΙΣΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("shall be insulted"), ΕΜΠΤΥΣΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("shall be spat upon"), ΜΑΣΤΙΓΩΣΑΝΤΕΣ ("having scourged"), ΑΠΟΚΤΕΝΟΥΣΙΝ ("they shall kill"). Each verb escalates the degradation. The sequence culminates in ΤΗ ΗΜΕΡΑ ΤΗ ΤΡΙΤΗ ΑΝΑΣΤΗΣΕΤΑΙ ("on the third day he shall rise") — the resurrection appended as the final item, grammatically parallel to the sufferings but theologically their reversal.

23. **18:34 — "they understood none of these things" (ouden toutōn synēkan):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΙ ΟΥΔΕΝ ΤΟΥΤΩΝ ΣΥΝΗΚΑΝ. The incomprehension is stated three ways with escalating force: ΟΥΔΕΝ ΤΟΥΤΩΝ ΣΥΝΗΚΑΝ ("they understood none of these things"), ΗΝ ΤΟ ΡΗΜΑ ΤΟΥΤΟ ΚΕΚΡΥΜΜΕΝΟΝ ΑΠ ΑΥΤΩΝ ("this saying was hidden from them"), and ΟΥΚ ΕΓΙΝΩΣΚΟΝ ΤΑ ΛΕΓΟΜΕΝΑ ("they did not comprehend the things being said"). The triple statement is unique to Luke among the Synoptics. The perfect passive participle ΚΕΚΡΥΜΜΕΝΟΝ ("having been hidden") implies divine concealment — their incomprehension is not merely cognitive failure but the result of something being veiled from them. This creates a theological tension: Jesus speaks plainly (the words are explicit about suffering and death), yet understanding is withheld. The disciples' blindness at this point sets up the ironic juxtaposition with the physical blindness healed in the following episode.

24. **18:35 — "as he drew near to Jericho" (en tō engizein auton eis Ierichō):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΩ ΕΓΓΙΖΕΙΝ ΑΥΤΟΝ ΕΙΣ ΙΕΡΙΧΩ. A notable discrepancy exists among the Synoptics: Luke places the healing as Jesus approaches Jericho (ΕΓΓΙΖΕΙΝ, "to draw near"), while Mark 10:46 places it as Jesus leaves Jericho (ΕΚΠΟΡΕΥΟΜΕΝΟΥ). Various harmonizations have been proposed (two Jerichos — old and new; different events; different vantage points), but the formal translation follows Sinaiticus without harmonization. The blind man is described as ΤΥΦΛΟΣ ΤΙΣ ("a certain blind man") — Luke does not provide the name Bartimaeus, which Mark preserves. The participle ΕΠΑΙΤΩΝ ("begging") — from ΕΠΙ + ΑΙΤΕΩ ("to ask upon, to beg") — indicates his means of survival. Blindness in the ancient world meant complete social and economic marginalization; begging was the only recourse.

25. **18:38-39 — "Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me" (Iēsou huie Daueid, eleēson me):** Sinaiticus reads ΙΗΣΟΥ ΥΙΕΔΑΥΕΙΔ ΕΛΕΗΣΟΝ ΜΕ. The title ΥΙΟΣ ΔΑΥΕΙΔ ("Son of David") is a messianic designation — it invokes the promise of 2 Samuel 7:12-16 that a descendant of David would reign forever. The blind man's use of this title is an act of confession: he recognizes Jesus as the Messiah. The crowd's rebuke (ΕΠΕΤΙΜΩΝ ΑΥΤΩ, "they rebuked him") and the command ΙΝΑ ΣΙΓΗΣΗ ("that he should be silent") attempt to suppress this confession. His response — ΑΥΤΟΣ ΠΟΛΛΩ ΜΑΛΛΟΝ ΕΚΡΑΖΕΝ ("he cried out much more") — uses the comparative ΜΑΛΛΟΝ ("more") intensified by ΠΟΛΛΩ ("by much"). Opposition increases rather than diminishes his appeal. The verb ΚΡΑΖΩ ("to cry out") denotes a loud, inarticulate shout — the desperate cry of one who has nothing to lose.

26. **18:40-41 — "What do you wish that I should do for you?" (ti soi theleis poiēsō?):** Sinaiticus reads ΤΙ ΣΟΙ ΘΕΛΕΙΣ ΠΟΙΗΣΩ. Jesus's question is not for information — the man's need is obvious — but for confession. By requiring the blind man to articulate his request, Jesus transforms him from a passive recipient into an active petitioner. The response ΚΥΡΙΕ ΙΝΑ ΑΝΑΒΛΕΨΩ ("Lord, that I may receive my sight") uses ΚΥΡΙΕ ("Lord") — a more exalted title than the crowd's ΙΗΣΟΥ ΝΑΖΑΡΑΙΕ ("Jesus the Nazarene" in v. 37). The verb ΑΝΑΒΛΕΠΩ ("to look up, to receive sight again") — from ΑΝΑ ("up, again") + ΒΛΕΠΩ ("to see") — carries the double sense of seeing again (restoration) and looking up (elevation of gaze). The blind man asks for both physical sight and spiritual vision.

27. **18:42-43 — "your faith has saved you" (hē pistis sou sesōken se):** Sinaiticus reads Η ΠΙΣΤΙΣ ΣΟΥ ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ ΣΕ. The identical formula appears in 17:19 (the grateful Samaritan leper) and in 7:50 (the sinful woman). The perfect tense ΣΕΣΩΚΕΝ ("has saved," with abiding result) again conflates physical healing and spiritual salvation. The command ΑΝΑΒΛΕΨΟΝ ("receive your sight," aorist imperative) effects the cure instantaneously. The result is threefold: ΠΑΡΑΧΡΗΜΑ ΑΝΕΒΛΕΨΕΝ ("immediately he received his sight"), ΗΚΟΛΟΥΘΕΙ ΑΥΤΩ ("was following him" — the imperfect denoting the beginning of continuous discipleship), and ΔΟΞΑΖΩΝ ΤΟΝ ΘΕΟΝ ("glorifying God"). The healed man becomes a disciple — the response Jesus sought from the rich ruler and did not obtain. The chapter closes with collective worship: ΠΑΣ Ο ΛΑΟΣ ΙΔΩΝ ΕΔΩΚΕΝ ΑΙΝΟΝ ΤΩ ΘΕΩ ("all the people, having seen, gave praise to God"). The blind man who now sees stands in contrast to the disciples who, in verse 34, could not understand — physical sight restored becomes a parable of spiritual illumination.

### Translation Philosophy

1. **Formal equivalence in parabolic discourse:** Luke 18 contains two major parables (the persistent widow, vv. 1-8; the Pharisee and tax collector, vv. 9-14) followed by three encounter narratives (children, vv. 15-17; rich ruler, vv. 18-30; blind man, vv. 35-43), with a passion prediction at the center (vv. 31-34). The formal translation preserves the structural connectives (ΔΕ, ΚΑΙ, ΕΙΠΕΝ ΔΕ, "and," "but," "and he said") that join these episodes, maintaining the Sinaiticus text's paratactic style rather than imposing subordination or thematic grouping.

2. **Preserving the Pharisee's prayer as self-directed speech:** The ambiguity of ΠΡΟΣ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ in 18:11 is a crucial interpretive hinge. The formal translation renders "praying these things to himself" to retain the possibility that the Pharisee's ostensible prayer to God is in fact a soliloquy of self-approval. This reading is supported by the content of the prayer, which mentions God only as audience for a recital of personal achievements.

3. **"Justified" as forensic declaration:** The translation of ΔΕΔΙΚΑΙΩΜΕΝΟΣ in 18:14 as "justified" preserves the Pauline resonance of the term — this is the only parable in the Synoptic Gospels that uses ΔΙΚΑΙΟΩ in the soteriological sense. The perfect passive participle indicates a completed divine action with continuing effect: the tax collector leaves the temple in an abiding state of divine acquittal.

4. **The structural irony of seeing and blindness:** Luke 18 ends with the healing of a blind man (vv. 35-43) immediately after the disciples' incomprehension (v. 34). The formal translation preserves the vocabulary linkage: the disciples ΟΥΚ ΕΓΙΝΩΣΚΟΝ ("did not comprehend") while the blind man ΑΝΕΒΛΕΨΕΝ ("received sight"). By maintaining the literal force of each verb, the translation allows the ironic contrast to emerge without editorial commentary.

5. **Continuity with Luke 17:** The chapter opens with a parable on prayer (18:1-8) that continues the eschatological discourse of 17:20-37. The question "when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith upon the earth?" (18:8) links the parable directly to the Parousia teaching of the preceding chapter. The formal translation preserves the connective ΕΛΕΓΕΝ ΔΕ ("and he was telling") that binds 18:1 to the preceding discourse, resisting any suggestion of a fresh beginning at the chapter division.
